sound_type: Maximum vocabulary display: an extended vocal session (5-20 minutes) in which an adult Raven demonstrates the full range of its acoustic repertoire -- alternating between territorial kronk calls (300-800 Hz), melodic courtship sequences, alarm series, begging calls, food recruitment calls, play vocalizations, mimicked sounds (wolf, other birds, environmental sounds), and experimental/novel vocalizations not categorized in the known repertoire. FIRST CORVIDAE IN ENGINE. 30+ documented distinct call types -- the largest avian vocal repertoire in the Pacific Northwest.
ecosystem: Open ridge, subalpine zone, coastal bluff, old-growth forest margin -- habitats that provide: (1) elevated sightlines for territorial surveillance; (2) thermal updrafts for effortless soaring (Ravens soar extensively); (3) acoustic propagation over open terrain. PNW contexts: mountain ridgelines (Cascades, Olympics), coastal headlands (Cape Flattery, Point Reyes-equivalent north coast bluffs), old-growth forest edges (Olympic Peninsula, Cascades old-growth patches), agricultural margins in eastern Washington.
season: Resident (year-round). Peak vocal complexity: October-March during courtship and territory establishment. Peak territorial calling: January-April during pair bonding and nest-site selection.
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Widespread and common throughout Holarctic. PNW population stable. Historical persecution (bounties in some states) has ended; populations have recovered in most areas.
milestone: FIRST CORVIDAE IN ENGINE. E=7. Corvidae is the most cognitively advanced bird family in the world. Corvus corax: largest Corvid, widest distribution (full Holarctic), most vocal (30+ call types), most cognitively documented. Theory of mind (Bugnyar et al.: food-caching re-cache when observed); tool use; future planning; play behavior (Bernd Heinrich multi-decade study). The Raven is not making noise -- it is thinking out loud. FIRST CORVIDAE opens the engine's most cognitively complex bird arc.

coordinates.description: Open ridge habitats: Olympic Peninsula ridgelines (Hurricane Ridge, 1,400m, consistent Raven territory), Cascades ridge systems (1,500-2,500m), coastal bluffs of Olympic Peninsula and San Juan Islands, old-growth forest margins. PNW Raven's most acoustically prominent positions: elevated ridgeline perches with open sightlines and acoustic propagation across valleys.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Maximum vocabulary display: extended session combining 30+ distinct call types. Major categories: (1) Territorial calls: deep resonant kronk/quork (300-800 Hz), produced from high perch or in flight, carrying 1 km+ across ridgelines. (2) Courtship melodic: bell-tone, water-drip, knocking, extended musical phrases -- the most melodically complex element of the Raven repertoire. (3) Alarm: rapid harsh series, higher frequency than territorial calls. (4) Food recruitment: specific call to recruit conspecifics to food resources. (5) Play vocalizations: soft, varied, distinct from all other categories. (6) Mimicry: wolf howls (documented wild behavior to recruit wolves to carcasses), other birds, environmental sounds, human speech elements (captivity). (7) Experimental/novel: unclassified vocalizations that do not fit known categories -- the Raven's acoustic improvisation.
acoustic_data.mechanics: Avian syrinx with dual-channel capability: the Raven, unlike the Red-tailed Hawk, exploits the two-bronchial channels to produce complex, simultaneous or rapidly alternating acoustic signals. The larger body (700-1,700g vs. RTH 690-1,460g) provides proportionally larger tracheal resonating structure. The result: a wider frequency range (territorial kronk at 300 Hz, alarm calls to 8 kHz) and greater dynamic range than any other PNW bird. The syrinx complexity relative to body size is comparable to or exceeds that of most songbirds.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: Full repertoire: 300 Hz - 8 kHz. Primary territorial call: 300-800 Hz (the characteristic deep resonant kronk). Courtship melodic calls: 500 Hz - 4 kHz. Alarm: 1-8 kHz. Mimicry: matches target species/sound frequency profile. The Raven occupies a wider acoustic frequency range than any other PNW bird documented thus far in the engine.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7 through cognitive density: (1) 30+ distinct call types -- the largest avian repertoire in the PNW; (2) maximum vocabulary display session (5-20 minutes) constitutes a high-information acoustic event; (3) amplitude: territorial kronk carries 1 km+ across ridgelines; (4) mimicry component: the Wolf mimicry documented in wild Ravens adds a cross-species strategic element unique in the engine; (5) cognitive dimension: the Raven's acoustic output is not instinctive-only but involves active cognitive processing (theory of mind, future planning, social intelligence). E=7 is the cognitive density calibration; the Raven does not reach E=8-9 because the dominant register is complexity, not volume/aggression.

nodes.title: FIRST CORVIDAE IN ENGINE -- The Cognitive Dimension Begins
nodes.description: Corvus corax is the first Corvidae entry. The family's arrival adds a cognitive dimension absent from all previous SPS entries: the vocalization is not just a sound but an expression of active cognition. Theory of mind, tool use, future planning, play behavior -- the Raven is the engine's first entry where the acoustic event is inseparable from the cognitive process producing it.

nodes.title: Raven Theory of Mind -- The Bugnyar Food-Cache Study
nodes.description: Thomas Bugnyar's research: Ravens that had cached food returned to re-cache it (move it to a new location) when they had been observed by another Raven. The re-caching behavior was not triggered by the presence of the observer but by the observer having seen the original cache location. This requires the caching bird to model the observer's knowledge state: 'that bird saw where I hid the food; I need to move it.' One of the strongest non-primate, non-ape demonstrations of perspective-taking and theory-of-mind precursors.

nodes.title: 30+ Distinct Call Types -- The PNW's Largest Avian Vocal Catalog
nodes.description: No other bird in the Pacific Northwest has a documented repertoire of 30+ distinct call types. The nearest competitors: American Crow (15-20 types), domestic/wild songbirds (10-20 types). The Raven's vocal catalog is not merely larger; it includes functionally distinct categories (food recruitment, wolf mimicry, experimental/novel) that represent cognitive vocal behaviors absent in lower-complexity avian species.

nodes.title: Wolf-Raven Vocalization Strategy -- Cross-Species Cognitive Tool Use
nodes.description: Wild Ravens documented mimicking wolf howls to call wolves to carcasses, then exploiting the carcass once the wolf has opened it. This cross-species behavioral strategy -- using vocal mimicry to recruit a mammal to do mechanical work -- is one of the most cognitively sophisticated wild animal behaviors documented in the PNW. The vocalization is not just communication but a tool for manipulating another species' behavior. The most complex single-vocalization behavior in the engine.

nodes.title: Bernd Heinrich Play Research -- Wild Ravens Play
nodes.description: Bernd Heinrich's multi-decade study of wild Raven behavior established play as a genuine behavioral category in wild Ravens: sliding down snow slopes, tug-of-war with sticks, dropping and catching objects in flight. Ravens play for its own sake. Play in non-human animals is associated with cognitive flexibility and behavioral innovation capacity. The Raven's play vocalizations are acoustically distinct from all other call types.

nodes.title: FIRSTS Pairing -- Raven + Alcohol Funnycar Both First Appearances at Degree 320
nodes.description: Both degree-320 entries are first appearances. Raven: FIRST CORVIDAE (maximum cognitive complexity, maximum vocabulary). Alcohol Funnycar: first Empty Records act (minimum label infrastructure, minimum song form, maximum punk economy). Both E=7. Cognitive density (30+ call types, 5-20 minute vocal session) vs. economic minimalism (2-minute songs, fast tempos, raw production). E=7 reached through opposite routes: maximum vocabulary vs. minimum form. The engine's clearest demonstration that a single energy level contains multiple acoustic philosophies.

cross_references.relationship: E=7 + E=7 = E=14. FIRSTS PAIRING: both first appearances. Cognitive maximum (Raven, 30+ call types) vs. economic minimum (AFC, 2-minute punk songs). Both E=7 through opposite routes: complexity vs. economy.

Consecutive E=7 bird entries. Accipitridae (RTH, territorial precision) immediately followed by Corvidae (Raven, cognitive complexity). Both E=7. Precision vs. cognitive density: the two avian E=7 modes documented in consecutive degrees.

Both cognitively complex species at acoustic maximum events: Orca E=9 through social pack maximum; Raven E=7 through individual cognitive maximum. Convergent cognitive evolution: corvids and cetaceans are the two most cognitively complex non-primate lineages. The engine documents them both.

cross_references.species: American Crow, Steller's Jay, Clark's Nutcracker, Canada Jay, Northwestern Crow
cross_references.relationship: FIRST CORVIDAE opens the family arc. All five other PNW Corvidae species are future entries. The Raven's entry establishes the cognitive register for the entire family: expect cognition documentation alongside acoustics in all Corvidae entries.
